1: Eleazar consecrated to guard the Ark at Kiriath-jearim — a private household arrangement for YHWH's dwelling. The dwelling of divine presence in imperfect, unofficial, provisional arrangements is a recurring pattern: YHWH meets with Moses at a burning bush, makes a covenant under the stars with Abraham, speaks in a still small voice to Elijah in a cave. The Son of God is born in a stable. Divine presence is not dependent on architecture.
2: Twenty years of the Ark at Kiriath-jearim, and "all the house of Israel mourned and yearned after the LORD." The long period of absence — presence nearby but inaccessible for regular worship — is the experience of exile: YHWH near but not accessible in the usual ways. The mourning and yearning describe the condition of the people before Samuel's revival. They map onto the condition of human longing for God that the Incarnation addresses: the Word making his dwelling among us (John 1:14) ends the long exile.
3: Samuel's call to return — "If you are returning to the LORD with all your heart, then remove the foreign gods" — is the covenant-renewal formula: full covenant loyalty requires the removal of competing allegiances. Jesus's formulation "you cannot serve God and mammon" (Matthew 6:24) is the same exclusive demand. The kingdom of God requires entire reorientation, not merely the addition of YHWH to an existing portfolio of loyalties.
4: Israel removes the Baals and Ashtaroth and "served the LORD alone" (wayyaʿaḇdû ʾet YHWH ləḇaddô) — the ləḇaddô (alone/only) formulation is the Deuteronomic summary of covenant faithfulness (Deuteronomy 6:4-5: "YHWH our God, YHWH alone"). The moment of singular loyalty to YHWH is the covenant precondition for the deliverance that follows (vv9-11). Faith that removes competing allegiances enables the divine warrior to act.
5: Samuel's intercession at Mizpah — "I will intercede for you before the LORD" (wəʾetpallel baʿadḵem ʾel YHWH). The priestly-prophetic role of intercessor is Samuel's most Christ-like function in these chapters. Hebrews 7:25 — "he always lives to make intercession for them" — describes Christ's permanent high-priestly function. Samuel's gathering and interceding for the people who had strayed from YHWH is a type of Christ gathering and interceding for the scattered covenant community.
6: The water-pouring before YHWH, fasting, and confession — "we have sinned against the LORD" — is the full penitential response. The water-pouring is not explained (a unique ritual here) and may symbolize the pouring out of the self before YHWH (cf. Psalms 22:14; Lamentations 2:19). The pouring out of self before God as the condition for receiving grace anticipates Philippians 2:7 — Christ "poured himself out" (ekened) in the Incarnation — and the NT's repeated call to self-offering (Romans 12:1).
7: The Philistines advance against the assembly at Mizpah — the covenant people gathered in penitence are immediately threatened. The pattern of the enemy attacking at the moment of covenant renewal is consistent throughout the OT (Exod 14: Pharaoh pursues the just-released Israel) and NT (the devil tempts Jesus immediately after his baptism, Matthew 4:1). Covenant commitment attracts opposition.
8: Israel asks Samuel: "Do not stop crying out to the LORD our God for us." The request for unceasing intercession reflects the community's recognition of their need for a mediator who can stand where they cannot. The appeal to Samuel reflects the same need addressed by Hebrews 4:16 ("we may with confidence approach the throne of grace") — access through a mediator rather than direct approach to the holy.
9: Samuel's burnt offering of a nursing lamb and his crying out to YHWH while under threat — the intercessor-priest combining sacrifice and prayer in the moment of crisis. The combination of sacrifice and intercession in a single figure performing both simultaneously is the fullest type of Christ's high-priestly work. Hebrews 9:24 — "he entered heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God for us" — is the antetype: the sacrificial act and the ongoing intercession united in one person.
10: "The LORD thundered with great thunder against the Philistines that day" (wayyarʿem YHWH bəqôl gādôl bayyôm hahûʾ). The divine-warrior thunder is the weapon that routs the enemy while Samuel's sacrifice is still in progress. The simultaneity — Samuel offering, YHWH fighting — demonstrates that intercession is the mechanism by which the divine warrior engages on behalf of the covenant people. The same structure governs Revelation 8:3-5: the prayers of the saints, offered before God with incense, return as fire cast on the earth.
11: Israel pursues and strikes down the Philistines following the divine intervention — human action as the follow-through after YHWH's decisive act. The pattern of divine initiative followed by human participation in the victory appears throughout the conquest narratives and reaches its eschatological form in Revelation 19:11-21 (Christ rides out to battle; the armies of heaven follow).
12: Ebenezer — "Thus far the LORD has helped us" (ʿad hēnnāh ʿăzāranû YHWH). The stone of remembrance marks a specific point in covenant history at which YHWH's faithfulness is attested. The phrase "thus far" implies there is more to come. The resurrection is the NT's Ebenezer: the definitive historical act of divine help that grounds all subsequent trust (Romans 8:32: "He who did not spare his own Son... how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?").
13: The Philistines subdued throughout Samuel's lifetime — the result of the covenant renewed at Mizpah. Samuel's tenure is a period of relative covenant faithfulness and divine protection. The pattern: covenant faithfulness brings covenant protection; covenant rebellion brings covenant curse. The entire DtrH is organized around this principle, which the NT reframes in Christ: the one who fulfilled the covenant perfectly secures the inheritance for those who are in him.
14: Territorial restoration — towns taken by the Philistines returned to Israel, peace with the Amorites. The scope of restoration following covenant renewal demonstrates that YHWH's faithfulness extends to political and territorial realities, not only to inner spiritual states. The eschatological restoration of "all things" (Acts 3:21) is the ultimate fulfillment of this pattern.
15: Samuel judged Israel throughout his lifetime — the faithfulness of a single covenant-faithful leader providing stable covenant administration for an entire generation. The longevity of Samuel's impact is a shadow of what Christ provides: a permanent mediator whose work does not expire with his mortality (Hebrews 7:23-24: Jesus holds his priesthood permanently because he lives forever).
16: Samuel's circuit ministry — Bethel, Gilgal, Mizpah — reaching the entire covenant community. The itinerant ministry of the covenant mediator, going out to where the people are, anticipates Jesus's Galilean circuit ministry (Matthew 4:23; 1 Samuel 9:35). The shepherd does not wait for the sheep to come to a fixed location but goes to where they are.
17: Samuel's base at Ramah, where he built an altar — the covenant mediator establishing a permanent worship site at his home. The altar at Ramah is his last piece of covenant infrastructure, connecting his prophetic ministry to the sacrificial system. The NT closes the era of specific altar sites: Jesus tells the Samaritan woman "neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem will you worship the Father" (John 4:21), because the true worship of God in Spirit and truth transcends every geographic center.
